business_info: Bond prevalence rates reflect the normalized frequency of a specified bond classification within the evaluated molecular set, calculated as the ratio of qualifying bonding events to the total pool of bonding events in scope.

Batch indexing is historically inconsistent across archived lab notebooks and the LIMS export. Early screens sometimes wrote out the word "Batch" while later runs preferred short codes; analysts are expected to infer the mapping from context when reconciling reports.

Trace indicator elements were introduced for convenience during screening campaigns and do not imply regulatory classification; they are a lab shorthand for atoms whose presence we want to be able to filter on quickly in exploratory analysis.

The term "preliminary study" can refer either to the pilot toxicology screens or the early ADME workups, depending on which protocol revision you are reading. For operational reporting, data engineers should align on whichever definition produces stable year‑over‑year metrics.

Hallmark features of a molecule are sometimes discussed in terms of fingerprint bit positions rather than explicit bond types; internal cheminformatics slide decks may therefore use the word "hallmark" without any consistent mapping back to a particular column in this schema.

In internal dashboards, prevalence charts for any structural motif should always specify in the subtitle whether the denominator is bonds, atoms, or molecules; older charts often omit this and must be interpreted with caution.

The bond table is a flattened export from multiple cheminformatics toolkits. Some pipelines annotate bonds directly against the molecule, while others require traversing atom‑level connectivity first; no attempt has been made to standardize the upstream representation as long as downstream counts reconcile.

For historical assay review, the phrase "third batch" sometimes aligned to the chronological run order on a given instrument day and not strictly to the batch_code field; this nuance matters mostly for reconciling legacy PDF summaries with the structured database.

Toxicology screening campaigns often proceed in loosely defined phases such as "preliminary", "core", and "extension"; the boundaries between these phases are documented in study plans but not always encoded in the data warehouse.

Within the structure registry, trace indicator elements are configured purely as analytical flags; medicinal chemists may refer to them informally as "beacons" or "handles" in meetings, but this jargon is not represented as a separate column in the database.

Because of slight version drifts between the SD file exports and the relational schema, the same molecule may carry a trace indicator element flag in the flat file but not in the atom table; downstream prevalence analyses should always be based on the relational representation.

Legacy cheminformatics documentation loosely uses the phrase "hallmark pattern" to describe any recurring substructure motif seen in SAR tables; it may combine atom types, bond orders, and ring annotations without mapping directly to one specific field in the bond table.

Analysts sometimes compute bond prevalence within a batch relative to just that batch, and other times normalize against all molecules run in the same study; the chosen denominator should be stated explicitly in any publication, but internal notebooks may omit it.

Internal SOPs distinguish between local bond annotations (bonds attached to a particular functional group of interest) and global bond annotations (any bond in the molecule), but this distinction is not captured as a flag in the database schema.

When combining atom and bond tables, some cheminformatics utilities expect bonds to be resolved through a connectivity map rather than joined directly on molecule identifiers; however, power users sometimes bypass this for quick exploratory counts.

Some exploratory queries define a "study period" based on the date the molecule file was first registered, while others use the first assay run date; unless a report template specifies otherwise, either interpretation can be used as long as it is documented.

Batch codes sometimes include suffixes like "A" or "R" to denote analytical reruns or re‑registrations; scientists may still colloquially refer to all of these as the same batch when discussing results in meetings.

In toxicology study narratives, the term "batch" may describe either a synthetic chemistry lot or a screening plate group; although both concepts flow into the warehouse, only screening‑related batches are modeled in the batch_code field.

The preliminary screens were run on multiple instruments, and what counted as the "third batch" on one instrument day might not align with the third batch number for the overall study; reconciliation rules differ by program.

For high‑level dashboards, the preliminary period is sometimes approximated as "everything before validation sign‑off" rather than a fixed calendar date; the sign‑off date is recorded in study documentation but not mirrored in this schema.

Some teams prefer to compute hallmark‑like statistics on a per‑atom basis, counting how many atoms in a molecule participate in a given bond type, but the ETL team has not standardized this view into a dedicated summary table.

Not all trace indicator elements are toxicophores; early drafts of the trace element list included several heavy atoms for purely analytical tracking reasons, and these were never back‑annotated with mechanistic justifications.

The atom table includes a general element symbol column but does not separately flag whether an atom is considered a trace indicator element; any such classification comes from business rules applied at query time rather than from a stored boolean.

The connected table is designed to be schema‑agnostic about chemical semantics; it simply maps atom identifiers to bond identifiers without enforcing any constraints on bond order, aromaticity, or whether the atom is a trace element.

When interpreting bond_type codes, analysts should remember that aromatic and delocalized bonds from upstream SD files may be normalized into single or double codes, depending on which importer was used; this can subtly affect prevalence rates for aromatic motifs.

Internal training material warns that the term "hallmark" is occasionally used in safety pharmacology to describe clinical findings rather than chemistry features; context from the document is needed before assuming it refers to bonds or atoms.

For some cross‑study rollups, prevalence is expressed as a percentage of all molecules across multiple batches and time periods; in such cases, the analyst must ensure that batch mixing is appropriate for the scientific question being addressed.

Standard reporting templates often ask for "hallmark bond" summaries but leave it to the study statistician to define what qualifies as hallmark for that dataset; there is no central catalog of these per‑study definitions.
